personal.name: Ángel Roldán Rabanal
personal.title: Multiplatform Application Developer
personal.tagline: Creative, curious developer, I like things that work beautifully in production. Mostly PHP, SQL and JavaScript on web and mobile; this site is my Next.js lab. Based in Seville; Erasmus+ in Prague was my 2026 international chapter. Always learning.
personal.closing: Driving licence · fine with travel when it makes sense, write if you want to compare notes or talk roles.

about: Passionate, creative multiplatform application developer, I completed the Higher Vocational Degree in Multiplatform Application Development at SAFA (May 2026). I ship web and mobile work with PHP, JavaScript, SQL and more: user-centred delivery is still the thread through my GitHub and paid roles. I love building things that work beautifully, learning fast, and contributing to real projects with genuine curiosity. I like systems that stay understandable, backends you can reason about, front-ends that do not fight the user, integrations you can reopen months later. Erasmus+ in Prague (Buinsoft, through May 2026) layered AI-assisted workflows and an international cadence on top of the same obsession: how software actually behaves in production.

projects.title: CampusGo
projects.whatIs: An individual academic project, but I treated it like a real product. Schools can post, edit and delete events. Students can browse them, subscribe, cancel, and pay for paid events. There is also a social layer, posting photos from past events, liking and commenting, and a ranking of the most popular events. Two roles, teachers and students, each with their own permissions and views.
projects.whatBuilt: I built CampusGo as two separate repos: an Angular and IONIC front for web and Android, and a Java backend with a REST API and MySQL. I wired the full event lifecycle on the school side and subscriptions, payments, and cancellations on the student side. I also added the social layer, photo uploads from past events, likes, comments, and a ranking of the most attended events, with separate permissions and views for teachers and students.
keyDecisions: Two separate repos, one for the web and mobile front in Angular and IONIC, one for the Java backend. Keeping them separate made it easier to work on both without breaking the other.
Java for the backend because it was what I knew deepest at the time and I wanted to build something I could trust.
IONIC let me ship the same codebase to web and Android without maintaining two separate projects.

projects.title: TechnoApp
projects.whatIs: A personal project I built because I wanted it to exist. A web app for techno fans where you can browse events, subscribe to them, and get AI-powered predictions on the music style for each venue that night. It also recommends events based on your preferences, lets you send friend requests and chat in real time. Built with Next.js and Firebase. Deployed on Vercel and still getting updates.
projects.whatBuilt: I built the full product in Next.js and Firebase: public routes for events, calendar, profile, social, and real-time chat, plus guarded admin tooling behind the scenes. I exposed APIs for events, venues, agenda, friendships, and chats, and I added an AI layer that reads user preferences and event data to predict the music style for each venue. I deploy on Vercel and keep shipping updates as I refine the experience.
keyDecisions: Firebase for auth, real-time chat and database without managing a backend myself. It let me focus on the product instead of infrastructure.
Next.js for fast page loads and easy Vercel deployment. The combination of both meant I could go from idea to deployed in a single day.

The AI layer was the part I enjoyed building most. It reads user preferences and event data to generate style predictions for each venue. It is the feature that makes the app feel different from a regular event listing.

projects.title: A Saturday Night of Angel
projects.whatIs: A 2D pixel-art platformer with a cyberpunk tone, built with Julián Navas Medina and Jose Antonio Campos Rueda for our Multimedia Programming course (2º DAM, 2025–26). The player fights or dodges enemies across three levels until reaching the bar, the goal. Along the way you pick up health and collectible pills. We organized everything in Unity with Tags and Layers so scripts, physics materials and collisions stayed predictable.
projects.whatBuilt: I led the team of three and kept the pieces compatible across MainMenu, three levels and the game-over / win flows. I built Level 2 and the main menu, and wrote the core C# scripts: player movement, double jump, shooting, health and respawn, enemy behaviour, level transitions, parallax scrolling (front, middle, back), DeathZone, camera follow, and life pickups with their HUD container. Julián handled Level 1, moving platforms and collectible logic; Jose Antonio designed the levels, built Level 3, wired menu / game over / end-game flows and added the music and SFX. We used Git from day one so we would not overwrite each other's scenes.

gallery.alt: Player character animations and movement in A Saturday Night of Angel
gallery.caption: Player, movement, double jump, shooting and animations tied to each action.

gallery.alt: Enemy sprites and behaviours in the 2D platformer
gallery.caption: Enemies, patrol, shoot in range, damage the player and react to hits.

gallery.alt: Level layout with HUD for health and collectibles
gallery.caption: Level & HUD, moving platforms, pickups, life UI and parallax background.

gallery.alt: Different enemy types including a turret with detection range
gallery.caption: Enemy variety, blockers, runners and a turret that only fires inside its range.

mechanics.title: Player
items: Horizontal movement, jump and double jump
Shoot weapon; lose and recover health
Damage enemies; fall off the map with respawn point
Pick up collectibles; animations for every action

mechanics.title: Enemies
items: Horizontal patrol; shoot when the player is in range
Movement animations; turret uses a detection radius so it does not shoot across the whole level

mechanics.title: Environment
items: Health pickups (+1 life) and pill collectibles
Death zone on falls; goal triggers next level or game end
HUD for life and collectibles; main menu, game over and win screens
Parallax background in three layers (front, middle, back)

tasks: Player, enemy, level-change, parallax, DeathZone and camera-follow scripts
Moving platform scripts and collectible / heal logic
Main menu actions, game over, end game and in-game flow

projects.challenges: C# and the Unity editor were new to all of us, prefabs, the Hierarchy, panels for game over, life and collectibles felt opaque at first. Scripts were the hardest part until we understood how Tags, Layers and physics materials connected.
projects.solutions: We relied on course documentation and classes, tutorials and AI assistants for basics, and constant knowledge-sharing between the three of us so we learned faster and retained more.

highlights.title: Cyberpunk pixel art
highlights.body: Retro web-game aesthetic, simple mechanics, strong mood. Not pixel-perfect, but cohesive and fun to play for hours.

highlights.title: Animation & audio
highlights.body: Character animations match every action. Original music and SFX make a simple game feel alive and much more immersive.

highlights.title: Enemy design
highlights.body: Several enemy types block the path, chase platforms or shoot, the turret only fires inside its detection range so it cannot snipe you from the end of the level.

keyDecisions: Tags and Layers everywhere, player, enemies, pickups and triggers stayed organized so scripts and physics stayed maintainable as scenes grew.
Pixel art on purpose: limited scope, finished look. We wanted a complete mini-game, not a greyboxed prototype.
First time leading a trio, coordination cost as much as coding. Git and clear ownership per level/system kept us from breaking each other's work.
When stuck on Unity or C#, we mixed official material, peer explanation and targeted AI help instead of guessing in isolation.

projects.title: PyMongo Project

gallery.alt: Rankings SAFA, game detail page with ratings and community reviews

projects.whatIs: An individual project to learn NoSQL data management. Built a Python app connected to MongoDB using PyMongo. Small scope but it changed how I think about data, understanding when a document model makes more sense than a relational one.
projects.whatBuilt: I built a Python app that talks to MongoDB through PyMongo with no ORM in between, just direct queries so I could see exactly what was happening to the data. I designed document shapes without a fixed schema and learned when that trade-off beats a relational model with joins. The scope stayed small on purpose; the payoff was understanding NoSQL from the inside, not shipping a large product.
keyDecisions: Choosing MongoDB over a relational database forced me to think differently about data structure. No joins, no fixed schema. It was uncomfortable at first and useful after.
PyMongo kept things close to the metal. No ORM, just direct queries, which helped me understand what was actually happening with the data.

experience.description: Built the release creation flow, multi-step form with dynamic validation and real-time preview, plus admin panels and client-facing views. Strong focus on user experience; tuned every feature and database call for shorter load times and better scalability.

Rebuilt two production websites in Next.js from scratch with AI-assisted workflows, buinsoft.com and itsalesaas.com. Also developed and published an Android app for buinsoft.com on the Play Store. First international mobile release, with careful attention to user experience across web and mobile.

Started as an intern and ended up hired on the team. Built internal web tools used across all SAFA schools in Andalusia, student reports, admin panels, and management views, using external APIs where needed. Prioritized user experience and kept features and database access as efficient as possible for shorter load times and greater scalability.

heading: A bit
about me
body: I'm Ángel, a multiplatform developer from Seville. I just finished my Higher Vocational Degree in Multiplatform App Development and I've been working on real projects since 2023, from institutional tools to a music platform. I like building things that actually get used. In early 2026 I did an Erasmus+ internship in Prague, which taught me a lot about working in an international team and about AI-driven development. I'm looking for a place where I can keep growing and contribute from day one.

teamBring.title: What I bring to a team
items: I'm comfortable leading and being led. I've worked in real teams where both happened in the same week.
Java is my strongest language. It's where I've gone deepest and where I feel most confident solving complex problems.
When I joined my first real project at SAFA I had to pick things up fast with no handover. I did, and I stayed three years.
What drives me is working on hard things. A complex problem gives me more energy than an easy task.
